html:
Leoch International Technology (842.HK): Failed Spin-Off, Real AI Power Exposure, Stub Valuation

Leoch is messy in exactly the way small-cap value usually gets mispriced. It just reported a 2025 loss, abandoned a planned U.S. spin-off, absorbed tariff disruption, ramped new capacity, and lost its dividend. The stock now trades at HKD 0.98, a roughly HKD 1.41 billion market cap, versus RMB 17.21 billion of 2025 turnover and RMB 4.44 billion of equity attributable to owners. That is about 0.07x sales, 0.28x book, and roughly 0.34x tangible book.

The market is treating Leoch like a broken lead-acid manufacturer. The better read is that 2025 was an ugly transition year inside a business with live exposure to one of the strongest infrastructure cycles in the world: data center backup power.

The core asset is reserve power batteries. In 2025, reserve power was RMB 7.16 billion of revenue, 41.6% of group sales, and still grew 4.5% despite tariff and customer disruption. Leoch says its network energy batteries rank second globally by sales revenue and its data center batteries rank first globally by sales, with continued wins from leading communications and data center customers in 2025. That claim matters because UPS and backup battery systems are not consumer batteries. Buyers value reliability, certification, lifecycle predictability, and supplier continuity more than fashionable chemistry.

The AI infrastructure cycle is not subtle. TrendForce now expects the top nine cloud service providers to spend about US$830 billion of capex in 2026, up 79%, with global data center installed power capacity reaching about 155 GW. Goldman Sachs frames the broader AI ecosystem investment opportunity in the trillions. The market has repriced obvious AI power winners, but not a Hong Kong-listed battery supplier with a tiny float, little institutional sponsorship, and almost no Western retail attention.

The variant perception is that lead-acid is not dead where Leoch actually makes its best money. Lithium is taking share in high-density and weight-sensitive applications, but data center backup is conservative. VRLA lead-acid remains cheap, proven, recyclable, and easy for engineers to model. Leoch also has lithium products, so the company is not purely defending an obsolete chemistry. It is selling backup power reliability across chemistries.

The 2025 loss was real, but not structurally fatal. The annual result showed revenue up 6.7%, but gross margin fell from 14.1% to 9.4%, driving a RMB 173.9 million attributable loss. Management blamed U.S. tariff changes, higher export costs, new product launches, new production-line commissioning, and short-term factory ramp inefficiency. The important forward detail is from the 2025 profit warning: affected product prices were not increased until the end of 2025, while the Mexico factory start was delayed from Q2 to Q4. That sets up 2026 as the first year where price adjustments and localized production can actually show up.

The operating leverage is large. On RMB 17 billion-plus of revenue, every 100 basis points of gross margin recovery is roughly RMB 170 million of incremental gross profit. A move from 9.4% to only 11.5% to 12.0%, still far below 2024, can plausibly swing Leoch back to RMB 200 million to RMB 300 million of net profit. A return toward 13% to 14% gross margin puts RMB 450 million to RMB 600 million back in play. At the current equity value, that is 4x to 6x base-case forward earnings and 2x to 3x bull-case normalized earnings.

The abandoned Leoch Energy spin-off is the misunderstood complexity. Leoch spent months preparing to list and distribute its global ex-China battery business in the U.S., then announced on April 1, 2026 that it would not proceed due to market and regulatory conditions. That was a failed catalyst, but it also means current Leoch shareholders still own the global business. Many data feeds and casual screens emphasize continuing operations revenue of RMB 10.57 billion, while the full group still had RMB 17.21 billion of 2025 turnover including the previously classified discontinued operation.

There is also an asset and control angle. The latest AGM confirms 1.442 billion shares outstanding and no treasury shares, while the company passed a 10% repurchase mandate. Founder Dong Li controls the company through Master Alliance, and insiders were buying stock in late 2025 above today’s price. That does not remove governance risk, but it does make a take-private, buyback, resumed dividend, or renewed separation attempt more plausible if operations stabilize.

The bear case is leverage and tariffs. Borrowings are high, finance costs were RMB 279 million in 2025, and the company had no final dividend. If tariffs remain chaotic, customers resist price increases, Mexico does not ramp efficiently, and gross margin stays near 9% to 10%, Leoch can remain a value trap. This is not a pristine compounder. It is a balance-sheet-heavy industrial recovery with real working-capital risk.

But the price already reflects a lot of that. Current LME lead is around US$2,000 per ton, broadly stable rather than a huge new input shock. The thesis does not need a lead price collapse or heroic AI hype. It needs current-cycle demand to persist, price/cost actions to work, and the market to stop valuing a global data center battery supplier at one-third of tangible book.

Bear target: HKD 0.55, down 44%, if gross margin fails to recover and leverage keeps the equity trapped.

Base target: HKD 2.00, up 104%, if Leoch earns RMB 200 million to RMB 300 million in 2026 or 2027 and rerates to roughly 0.55x book.

Bull target: HKD 3.50, up 257%, if gross margin normalizes near prior-cycle levels, reserve power accelerates with AI data center demand, and the market values Leoch closer to tangible book or 7x to 8x normalized earnings.
